data.title: NewLake Capital Partners: A Dirt-Cheap REIT With Real Potential
data.description:
Before my preliminary analysis, I didn't know much about NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP). I knew it was a REIT focused on the cannabis industry, and that alone raises a red flag, not to mention that it's a small cap. But on the stock's main page, you can see a forward yield of 11.6% and a price-to-FFO ratio of 7.8x.

So, as I delved deeper into the thesis, I went hunting for where the big problems really were that caused the stock to trade so cheaply even with solid fundamentals.

And I found them. There really are important yellow (or even red) flags to consider, but the asymmetry is so good and the company is so robust that the rating is still buy with a constructive outlook.

Why NLCP is a Solid Company

The chart below shows much of what we need to know about the financials of a REIT, and it was one of the first things that caught my attention, although the history is relatively short. Revenue has shown steady growth in recent years, as has FFO per share, since the chart of outstanding shares also does not show a trend of constant dilution of shareholders.

In addition to not diluting shareholders, debt is minimal. NLCP's debt/equity ratio is less than 2%, while this indicator commonly exceeds 70%, as is the average for some of the company's "non-cannabis REIT peers," such as NNN, PSTL, VICI, FCPT, NTST, and EPRT. The valuation is also much better, but I will address that in another section.

Other big numbers also tell us a positive story. The dividend yield of almost 12% is with a healthy payout, with the company's target being an AFFO payout ratio between 80% and 90%. In other words, not only is this dividend yield not artificial, it has been sustained by an increase in AFFO, not just by an increase in payout.

The business model—except for its dependence on the cannabis industry—is pretty solid. 100% triple net leases, long-term contracts, most tenants are public and have positive EBITDA, and there are some interesting triggers related to the cannabis market.

In other words, it is quite easy to envision a scenario where FFO continues to increase year by year due to contracts, capital allocation, and some positive surprises that may occur.

The Relevant Yellow Flags

Even with this solidity, it is undeniable that the future is not so reliable. Can NLCP be a compounder (in the sense of being able to allocate capital well and generate an ever-increasing FFO)? Yes, certainly, its business model allows for this, and the little history we have shown us that the execution is good.

However, there are many uncertainties that go beyond the company. Despite choosing good tenants to work with, they can still cause problems due to the complexity and sensitivity of the market. Consider that a change in legislation, competition in a particular area, and the like can create a problem and render the tenant insolvent.

And that's where the uncertainties begin. In addition to the strong correlation with the industry itself, there is a dependence on some tenants. Most tenants represent at least 5% of NewLake Capital Partners' revenue, and some, such as Curaleaf (OTCPK:CURLF), still represent 22%.

Of the three main tenants - Curaleaf, Trulieve (OTCQX:TCNNF), and Cresco Labs (OTCQX:CRLBF) - all have a negative profit margin, but still manage to be good tenants because they already generate substantial cash flow and are not overly indebted. Still, having high exposure to companies with compressed margins that are not yet so consolidated makes the thesis riskier.

Some cases have even "gone wrong" and required some intervention. Calypso, for example, resumed paying rent, but was facing significant financial challenges until recently. REV Clinics, on the other hand, is a more critical case, with the company having entered receivership, paying only 50% of the rent, and NLCP expected to repossess the property at the end of Q2. Revolutionary Clinics accounts for 5.6% of revenue, so in a scenario of prolonged vacancy, earnings may be compressed for a while.

These cases are far from being the end of the world, but if something more widespread occurs in the industry, it could be very bad for shareholders, with even more aggressive deratings of multiples accompanied by a reduction in FFO. And there is a lack of clarity for the short term, as mentioned in the earnings call:

As we discussed just two months ago, we anticipated continuing headwinds for the cannabis sectors, the industry awaits key catalyst. Since then, we've unfortunately seen additional headlines reflecting distressed in the sector."

All of this should be considered when investing in NLCP. For example, even though it is an interesting stock, I do not believe it will do well in a more conservative income portfolio, at least not with a representative percentage.

Any Good News is Great News for NLCP

Using a Gordon model with the following assumptions, it is possible to find an upside of 17%:

  • Next year's dividend: $1.72
  • Discount rate: 10%
  • Annual dividend growth rate: 0%

The result of the equation is a fair value of $17.20 per NLCP stock. This means that if the forward dividend is similar to market estimates, and the company does not grow this dividend, the total return per year would be 10% if the stock were purchased for $17.2. As this is not the case, this implied IRR is even higher.

Note how assuming a 0% growth rate is conservative. Although there are the uncertainties mentioned, potential revenue losses for a certain period, it is not as if revenue will fall by a large amount and remain so for a long period. It seems to me to be much more of a temporary situation, where challenges are emerging that the company needs to overcome but that it will eventually continue to allocate capital well and be able to exploit its property portfolio.

And in a more normalized scenario where these challenges diminish, making FFO grow at a rate of at least low single digits does not seem so difficult (due to the triple net lease model, long-term contracts, and industry growth).

As shown in the first image of the analysis, both the price-to-FFO and price-to-book ratios are very low, already showing us a high asymmetry and how much the market discounts this REIT due to the challenges and uncertainties ahead.

There is a saying that "no news is good news," and adapting this a little to NLCP, I believe that any good news can be great news. In my view, the valuation is so compressed that positive catalysts for the industry (as the image below shows), improved industry sentiment, lower interest rates, and other internal triggers such as tenant recovery and stabilization with REV and Calypso properties may not only be good for shareholders but also unlock significant upside in the short/medium term.

If the market has more clarity for NLCP and believes that it is valid to pay at least 10x FFO, the upside is 28%. Note that at this multiple, the dividend yield could still be 8% if the company continued to distribute something close to 80%, i.e., it is a considerable upside and still far from making the company overvalued.

The Bottom Line

Given the above information, it is already possible to say that NLCP is a REIT that has surprised positively. There are many risks and uncertainties, but they are mitigated by a very compelling valuation, solid fundamentals, and potential positive surprises. Therefore, my rating for this REIT is a buy.

data.title: Peak Tesla? Market Share Erosion, Robotaxi Safety Issues And Overstretched Valuations
data.description:
Performance Assessment

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA)(TSX:TSLA:CA) has underperformed the S&P500 since my last 'Neutral/Hold' update on the stock:

Thesis

I am becoming bearish on Tesla:

  1. Tesla's automotive business is losing market share across the globe
  2. Tesla's robotaxis and FSD capabilities are under regulatory scrutiny for safety concerns
  3. TSLA stock is trading at peak valuations despite deteriorating earnings fundamentals
  4. TSLA stock is having trouble breaking past monthly resistances vs SPX500
  5. India seems like a high potential market, but the odds of successful penetration are low

Tesla's automotive business is losing market share across the globe

United States (~50% of revenues)

The Q1 2025 YoY comps show Tesla's EV sales volumes declining 9%, whereas many other competitors growing much faster:

Now, of course, a lot of the higher YoY gains from Tesla's automotive competitors may be attributed to a smaller base effect. But the data shows that despite EVs getting more popular, Tesla is still losing market share in the broader auto market:

Tesla’s sales peaked in the U.S. in the spring of 2023 when it pushed more than 173,000 EVs onto the roads, and its share of the total U.S. auto market hit 5%. That was then. In Q1 2025, the EV pioneer was heading back to earth: Sales of 128,000, down 26% from the peak and with a market share closer to 3%.

- Cox Automotive Analyst Stephanie Valdez Streaty in the Q1 Cox Automotive forecast call

So I think there are indications that Tesla's first mover advantage is fading away in the US.

China (~22% of revenues)

As the chart above shows, Tesla's EV market share in China is also steadily ticking down. This is because its competitors BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), XPeng (XPEV) (OTCPK:XPNGF) and NIO (NIO) are releasing more frequent product updates, often at better price points.

Europe (~18% of revenues)

In the EU, passenger car registrations rose 1.6% YoY in May 2025:

But Tesla's sales in the EU are in a slump, having fallen for 5 consecutive months. Registrations for its cars have dropped 40.5% YoY in May 2025, leading to a market share shrinking to 0.9% from 1.6% last year. This is despite a Model Y product redesign.

I suspect that the 8-10% price hike in the refreshed Model Ys are losing to cheaper Chinese EVs in Europe.

Tesla's robotaxis and FSD capabilities are under regulatory scrutiny for safety concerns

Tesla launched its driverless robotaxi ride-hailing service in Austin recently and although it got mostly positive reviews, there are some issues we should be aware of:

Firstly, the FSD technology used in the launch was custom-made to Austin and a Tesla employee was present in each vehicle as a safety monitor just in case manual intervention was required. Note that competitor Waymo does not issue any person to act as a safety monitor, and it also operates in a lot more cities. So this highlights scalability constraints for Tesla's robotaxis in the near term:

The use of an Austin-specific tech stack, a Tesla employee being present in the vehicle, and the navigation/lane issue reported in the first day of use suggests scaling will be slow in the near term

- Goldman Sachs analyst Mark Delaney, Author's bolded highlights

Secondly, the lane and navigation issues highlighted above speak to safety concerns of Tesla's robotaxis and FSD technology. On the very first day of its robotaxis launch, there were many reports of the robotaxis exceeding speed limits, breaking lane discipline and even running traffic lights. This has prompted US auto safety regulators such as the U.S. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) to investigate the matter further. Tesla is trying to keep details of these investigations away from the public, arguing that:

Nefarious actors could also use the marked information to smear Tesla's brand for the sake of notoriety. Absent public disclosure, access to the marked information and all of the knowledge gained from it would require significant expenditure of time and resources and very intimate knowledge about Tesla.

- Tesla regulatory senior counsel Casey Blaine

But I think this carries the risk of further public distrust, especially after Musk's polarizing involvement in the political sphere.

Frankly, these reports of safety issues are unsurprising to me, as I have previously noted that Tesla's FSD technology had run into similar rule-breaking incidents in a trial run last month. Also, according to aggregate data from Tesla FSD tracker, in the latest 13.x FSD models, lane issues and traffic control issues pop up as the highest causations for disengagement (the need for a human to take over):

Look at the left part of the image above to see the low proportion of 'NoDE's (no disengagement) in many vision-impaired conditions. This has drawn scrutiny over Tesla's approach to rely exclusively on cameras rather than the more expensive but effective LIDAR technology. Now, whilst I am long term bullish on Tesla's autonomous driving vision, I believe that in a bid to catch up to Waymo faster, Tesla may be speeding too fast and taking on a higher risk of crashes (literally). I can't help but wonder if the cameras-only approach to autonomous driving needs to spend a lot more collecting data in a wide variety of conditions before being deployed for public use.

TSLA stock is trading at peak valuations despite deteriorating earnings fundamentals

TSLA stock is trading at a steep valuation of 153x 1-yr fwd PE. That's a hefty 136.2% premium over its ~3-yr median of 65x:

Moreover, whilst the valuation multiple has appreciated peaking levels, the underlying earnings growth expectations have declined:

I think this is a signal of a clearly overvalued stock, especially if you consider the weakening market positioning of its existing automotive business and arguably critical issues with the new robotaxi operations.

TSLA stock is having trouble breaking past monthly resistances vs SPX500

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of TSLA vs SPX500

TSLA vs SPX500 has failed to break a couple of major monthly resistances. So I think it is due for a move down in search of more interested buyers at lower levels.

India seems like a high potential market, but the odds of successful penetration are low

At first glance, India looks like a very attractive market to enter. The country has great macro stats with its 1.5 billion population. The Indian EV market is expected to grow at a high 49% CAGR this decade. So I can understand the bullish optimism around Tesla's step into this promising market via showroom openings in 2 megacities; New Delhi and Mumbai. Indeed, this is a meaningful upside risk scenario to a bearish view on TSLA. But here are some very real challenges in successfully penetrating this attractive market (on paper):

History shows that cracking the Indian market is not easy for foreign automotive players. Many leading auto companies have tried but failed:

Companies like General Motors, Ford, Nissan, and Fiat have either left the market or struggled to keep up with domestic players.

- Automotive professional Reghuram C in a LinkedIn post

The key reasons is that the Indian market has some peculiar attributes that make it very difficult for auto companies much of the rest of the world to adopt the same playbook in expanding in India. Here's a table outlining the unique characteristics of the Indian automotive market and its implications and how Tesla is positioned plus what it needs to do to adjust:

Factor

India's unique characteristic

Implications

Tesla's positioning

Consumer preferences

Affordability, fuel-efficiency, low-maintenance tend to be far more important factors than safety, comfort and high performance

Smaller, budget cars are preferred

Even Tesla's mass-market models 3 and Y are priced at a premium, making it more suitable to affluent and rich buyers in India.

Tax incentives

High 70% import duties for foreign cars that are not made domestically in India

Competitive disadvantage vs local players

Tesla's showroom launch will be made of imported cars, but it has made initial steps to set up local manufacturing via a proposal for a $2-3bn manufacturing plant that can make up to 500k vehicles per year.

Infrastructure support

Highly congested roads, many narrow roads, limited parking

Larger vehicles are less practical in some regions

Tesla's Model 3 and Y are both larger than the standard Indian models. Tesla may need to create an India-specific model to expand its customer TAM.

Competition

The market is dominated by local players Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, Mahindra and Hyundai (one of the few foreign auto OEMs that has integrated well in India)

Tough barriers to entry for foreign players

Tesla needs to follow the same playbook it did in the US (with the launch of Model S and X) to build its presence in India by targeting niche rich and affluent customers first. Still, I suspect the transition to mass-market appeal will take a lot longer.

Takeaway & Positioning

Tesla's valuations are at peak levels despite deteriorating earnings fundamentals. The operational execution momentum is also faltering as the company is losing market share in all major markets. The robotaxis' potential argument has been a popular argument for the bulls, but I think the data shows some concerning signs of safety issues, which suggests that Tesla may be hurrying too much to commercialize its FSD technology in an attempt to catch up to competitors such as Alphabet's Waymo.

A new bullish hope has also recently emerged that maybe Tesla can significantly expand its TAM by entering the market with the largest population in the world - India. However, I think the odds of success here are heavily against Tesla's favor due to many idiosyncratic challenges. Of course, Musk has a track record of consistently beating the odds. And as the late, great Charlie Munger of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (BRK.A) has advised (in reference to Musk):

Never underestimate the man who overestimates himself. These weird guys who overestimate themselves occasionally knock it right out of the park.

- Charlie Munger

So I acknowledge there is a clear upside risk that Tesla may execute in India successfully. Still, that is not enough to change my overall 'Sell' stance on the stock.

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Neutral/hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: Black Hills: How Defensive Is Your Utility?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

To mitigate tariffs and geopolitical risks, I focus on stocks nearing inflection points, with tailwinds strong enough to cut through the macroeconomic noise.

On the other hand, many investors are rushing toward utilities to shelter from expected volatility. Surging AI data center demand adds a growth dimension, renewing interest in the sector. One example is Black Hills Corporation (NYSE:BKH).

This piece concludes that BKH

  1. isn't the optimal investment to capitalize on the AI data center trends
  2. isn't the most effective hedge against market volatility

BKH Has Historically Underperformed During Recessions and Economic Disruptions

During recessions and economic turbulence, BKH typically underperforms its peers - represented by the S&P 500 Utilities Sector Index (XLU). More importantly, its value as a market hedge is almost non-existent when compared to the S&P 500 index (SPX) and the Russell 3000 (RUA).

Period: 2001 dot-com bubble

BKH underperformed XLU, RUA, and SPX

Period: 2008 Great Recession

BKH underperformed XLU and performed in line with SPX and RUA

Period: 2015 shale-boom-bust

BKH underperformed XLU, SPX, and RUA

Period: Q1 2020 Coronavirus Pandemic

BKH underperformed XLU and marginally outperformed SPX and RUA

Profitability

Nearly 94% of BKH's assets are regulated, creating predictable returns. State Energy Commissions set the electric and gas rates that BKH can charge households. The rates are reviewed annually, ensuring that BKH investors receive a fair return on assets "ROA", running between 2.7% and 2.9% since 2020, as shown in the chart below.

BKH needs to ask permission from regulators before expanding its asset base. The capital structure (equity vs debt) is also regulated by the state. Through authorized leverage, BKH now realizes between 8% and 10% Return on Equity "ROE".

Given Inflation, is BKH's 4.7% Yield Worth It?

Some investors are questioning the value of BKH's 4.7% dividend yield given inflation rates, which currently stand at roughly 3%, and the returns on the 10-year treasury, which stand around 4.4%.

The dividend spread - BKH's dividend yield minus 10-year Treasury yield - is only 0.3%, a narrow equity risk premium. The inflation-adjusted real yield is 2.7% and has historically fallen below zero in periods of high inflation, as shown in the chart below.

However, this approach to measuring the value of BKH's return is flawed because it doesn't account for the earnings that BKH generates but keeps funding its growth.

A more suitable measure of real returns to shareholders is the earnings yield - (EPS/Share Price) which currently stands at roughly 7.9%, demonstrating a reasonable compensation for shareholders, even when accounting for inflation and risk-free rates. This is expected because State and Federal Energy Commissions incorporate inflation and risk-free rates when establishing the authorized ROE rates for BKH.

The Data Center Growth Myth

Some analysts hype BKH's growth prospects, pointing to rising demand from AI data centers. Although data centers are expanding and consuming more energy, framing this as a major growth driver is misleading.

First, none of the states that BKH operates is particularly a data center hub. Wyoming, South Dakota, and Montana aren't on the digital economy map, while Colorado is a secondary data center market at best.

Second, BKH's strategic positioning doesn't align with the clean energy mandates of many large corporations. The company generates more than a third of its electricity from coal and owns a coal mine. Data center operators in Colorado appear more enthusiastic about partnering with Xcel Energy than BKH. Xcel's target long-term EPS growth is between 6% - 8%, double the growth target of BKH, despite having a slightly above payout ratio.

Third, the contribution of data centers to BKH revenue is too small at this point, and its growth will barely move the needle and will stay as such for at least the next 5 years. BKH expects data center contribution to earnings to double between now and 2028, rising exponentially to potentially reach 10% of revenue in 2030. This suggests a modest current contribution.

Sustainable Growth Rate

The sustainable level of growth is essentially the level of the authorized ROE multiplied by the retained earnings. The company targets a payout ratio of 55% to 65%, retaining 35% to 45%. Based on an authorized ROE between 9% and 10%, the company's sustainable EPS growth falls between 3.15% and 4.5%. That's exactly what the company told investors to expect.

Dividend Stability

Generally, if BKH's expenses rise, it can pass the costs to the consumer. As long as management remains competent and compliant with risk mitigation guidelines, BKH will recover whatever cost it incurs, plus an agreed-upon return, enough to keep investors (and consumers) happy. This balance is critical to ensure continuous, reliable, and safe electric and gas service.

The main risk is the timing of unexpected costs and utility rate hikes to recover these costs. This timing gap creates profitability fluctuations, which, in rare cases, have been significant. Currently, BKH has multiple applications pending to raise prices to recover past costs it incurred last year. However, Moody's analysts note that BKH operates in states that are responsive to utility companies' needs. Despite some contention, and even protests from some town councilors in the past, I think the company has a good relationship with Energy Commissions, as evident in the rate increases that led to these town council-level protests in the first place.

A case in point is BKH's coal assets, which represent a third of its electricity generation capacity. In most sectors, this exposure would be worrying. But here? Not so much. Every plant is authorized by regulators and almost guaranteed a fair ROE via rate reviews. If regulators wish to transition to more clean energy, BKH has the talent to embark on such initiatives, fully paid for by households and commercial consumers.

Final Thoughts and Risks

BKH perhaps trades at the lower bound of the industry range, with a Valuation Quant Score of B+, but I wouldn't particularly call it undervalued. Compared to many of its peers, the lack of a clear sustainable energy roadmap excludes it, or at least puts it back in the line of many data center operators' procurement processes.

In terms of growth, the company has a Growth Quant Score of D, putting it in the 86th percentile among 53 of its peers in the gas, electric, and multi-utility industries. For this reason, I don't believe that BKH is undervalued, nor do I believe that we should expect an imminent expansion in its valuation multiples. The main risk to this hypothesis is a structural and sustainable shift in the environmental policies and attitudes in the United States, particularly towards legacy operators with stable generation capacity, such as BKH.

data.title: Whispers Of Cuts, Roars Of Chips
data.description:
Market Report for Thursday, June 26, 2025

Markets rallied Thursday, brushing off Washington’s procedural theater and the fading fiction that anyone still tracks the Byrd Bath, in favor of what really matters: AI, chips, and the transformation that’s no longer coming - it’s here.

Level Change 6/26/25 (%)

+0.9 Dow
+1.0 Nasdaq
+0.9 Nasdaq 100
+0.8 S&P 500
+1.3 S&P 400
+1.6 S&P 600

Washington kept up appearances with high drama over the reconciliation bill, still marinating in Senate procedure. Medicaid cuts, SALT caps, and the legislative equivalent of ghost pepper seasoning had everyone sweating. Don’t fall for it: passage is all but baked in. They’ll get their overspending done.

In a parallel theater, the administration hinted at an imminent trade twist, in which the EU relaxes both tariffs and non-tariff barriers. Treasury, in turn, scrapped its oddly personal “revenge tax” in exchange for G7 allies shelving their own retaliatory levies. The press secretary, in full Zen mode, waved off the July 9 deadline as more suggestion than schedule.

But the real signal came from the Fed, or rather, from the White House’s interpretation of it. President Trump is still considering naming a replacement for Chair Jerome Powell months before his term ends next May. The Fed chair, having stubbornly clung to his independence and economic models, now faces political replacement therapy.

Fedsters played it cool in monetary Esperanto.

Goolsbee said early naming wouldn’t rattle the committee. Daly and Collins advised patience. Barkin warned that trade policy resolution could drag on. Barr said the Fed is positioned to wait. The question is: wait for what? It used to be data. Now it looks more like they’re waiting for the Oval hammer to fall on Powell’s head. Adjustable-rate mortgage morons want him gone, data be damned.

But none of that really matters. Not the timing of cuts, not who chaired what. The only story with shelf life is AI.

Which brings us to Micron (MU -1.3%), which beat and raised on strong demand for DRAM and HBM chips. DRAM is the workhorse memory that brings multitasking to life. HBM - high-bandwidth memory - is the newer, faster, fancier cousin, built for AI models that like their data served hot and in bulk.

Micron’s earnings call was basically a mixtape for the AI crowd: accelerating demand, limited supply, pricing power. The market responded by bidding up the entire chip complex, with Nvidia (NVDA) touching another record again. No one remembers recession calls when AI chips are printing cash and guidance is going vertical.

And that was the day: Powell threatened, Powell ignored, tariffs teased to ease, and AI marching oblivious to it all. The macro crowd might notice when it’s Fed Chair GPT.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Calamos Hedged Equity Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy Overview

Calamos Hedged Equity Strategy’s investment approach, which blends a core long-equity portfolio with an actively managed options overlay, can be highly responsive to dynamic market conditions and serve as a portfolio diversifier. The strategy is often considered alongside more systematic or defined outcome peers. Defined outcome products are designed to capture a certain amount of downside or upside each quarter, depending on where the market moves. However, there are disadvantages to not being nimble in these turbulent markets. A significant drawback is a capped upside that cannot cover successive losses to the downside.

Compared to our mechanistic peers, the Calamos tactical management approach attempts to generate alpha via option market dynamics and equity market volatility—exploiting these opportunities by being favorably positioned for many outcomes. This approach instills confidence in the strategy, demonstrating our proactive stance and ability to capitalize on the market’s shifting gears.

Market Environment

During the first quarter, markets again took investors on a roller coaster ride. The market’s anticipation of business- and market-friendly policies (e.g., lower taxes and less regulation) gave way to anxiety about the impact of tariffs on inflation, consumer activity, and economic growth. The S&P 500 fought back from a 10% peak-to-trough decline but finished down more than 4% in the quarter, with a significant rotation away from tech stocks. After a disappointing 4Q loss and a lackluster 2024, the bond market made its way back into investors’ good graces, benefiting from the market’s flight to areas of the market with perceived safety.

Performance Review

The Calamos Hedged Equity Strategy demonstrated robust performance during the unprecedented volatility of Q1 2025. The pronounced volatility of the first quarter was driven by a confluence of factors: persistent inflation concerns, the implementation of new tariff policies, ongoing geopolitical tensions, and a significant rotation away from technology stocks. In our view, this market environment— characterized by rapid sentiment shifts and sector volatility—created precisely the conditions where our asymmetric hedging strategy demonstrates its full value to investors.

Performance Highlights:

Q1 2025: The strategy returned 0.61% (gross of fees) and 0.49% (net), outperforming the S&P 500 Index (SPX)(SP500) return of -4.27% and highlighting the effectiveness of our hedging strategy in preserving capital when it matters most.

Year: The strategy delivered a return of 6.85% (gross of fees) and 6.35% (net).

Risk Management: The portfolio registered a modest 0.32 beta versus the S&P 500 Index over the three-year period.

Positioning and Portfolio Changes

The strategy prioritizes total return with reduced volatility by investing in equity securities and utilizing an options overlay to enhance risk management. Our hedge strategy aims to position the portfolio for a wide range of outcomes, acknowledging the inherent uncertainty of future market conditions. This principle is particularly relevant in today’s environment.

The core of our portfolio remains the actively managed 65/35 option trade established in the latter half of 2023. This trade is designed to capture 65% of the market’s upside while limiting exposure to 33% of the downside, with an expiration date in December 2025. As active managers, we have made incremental adjustments to this hedge. In the current quarter, we traded puts around our core hedge to capitalize on short-term market volatility.

Risk Management

Effective risk management is central to our strategy. We employ several key techniques to mitigate risks:

  • Diversification: By investing across various sectors and asset classes, we reduce the impact of adverse movements in any one area.
  • Options Overlay: The dynamic options overlay allows us to hedge against potential market downturns while still participating in upward movements.
  • Active Management: We continuously monitor and adjust our positions to respond swiftly to changing market conditions.
  • Stress Testing: We conduct regular stress tests to help ensure our portfolio can withstand extreme market scenarios.

Outlook

Q1 2025 marked a successful quarter for CIHEX. Strong returns and effective risk management have set the stage for continued performance in the coming year. The portfolio’s approach, strategically blending a core long-equity portfolio with an actively managed option overlay, has allowed the portfolio to capitalize on market opportunities. It is currently well positioned to navigate dynamic market conditions, aiming for significant upside while limiting downside exposure.

For additional information, please visit the strategy’s profile page:

https://www.calamos.com/strategies/hedged-equity/

Past performance does not guarantee or indicate future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. Portfolios are managed according to their respective strategies, which may differ significantly in terms of security holdings, industry weightings and asset allocation from those of the benchmark(s). Portfolio performance, characteristics and volatility may differ from the benchmark(s) shown.

All portfolio positioning and sector information is for a representative portfolio.

For Institutional Use Only

The information portrayed is for the Calamos Hedged Equity Strategy. The inception dates and performance results shown are for the composite. Representative holdings and portfolio characteristics are specific only to the portfolio shown at that point in time. Other portfolios will vary in composition characteristics, and will experience different investment results. The representative portfolio shown has been selected by the advisor based on account characteristics that the advisor feels accurately represent the investment strategy as a whole.

data.title: Calamos International Growth Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Summary

  • As long-term investors, we seek to identify inflection points and position the strategy to capitalize on secular growth themes and disruption.
  • As fiscal spending shifts from the US to abroad, investors are becoming increasingly excited about the potential for a pick-up in growth outside the US. A sideways to downward move in the US dollar further supports this case.
  • The strategy remains diversified across a range of cyclical and secular themes, and we are excited that the breadth of opportunities has expanded across the global market, as previously out-of-favor areas of the markets enjoy renewed interest.

Market Environment

During the first quarter of 2025, global markets sharply and quickly reflected the possible commencement of an inflection in investor sentiment to favor ex-US markets over the long-preferred US market. After years of US and foreign investors building up exposure to the US market, tariff and fiscal policy developments may be catalysts for capital migration out of the US and into ex-US markets at the margin.

Global equities declined in the first quarter while experiencing a wide range of returns across regions. During the period, investors navigated an environment of heightened policy uncertainty, slowing growth, and a weaker US dollar. The broad-based MSCI ACWI Index returned -1.22% during the quarter. Developed markets returned -1.68%, as measured by the MSCI World Index, and US equities trailed in the period with a -4.27% return, as reflected by the S&P 500 Index (SPX)(SP500). Emerging markets outperformed in the quarter, as the MSCI Emerging Markets Index returned 3.01%.

US equities’ shares saw periods of spiking volatility and sold off as investors’ confidence faltered amid slowing growth and elevated policy uncertainty on multiple fronts. From an earnings perspective, the US continued to deliver solid results in the first quarter but also saw many management teams offer more guarded guidance for the periods ahead. Regarding the broad economy, recent data pointed to more muted growth conditions, while multiple measures also showed a stabilizing inflation trend. PMI figures reflected healthy demand overall, although business surveys reflected slowing production and new orders within manufacturing. With respect to monetary policy, the Federal Reserve kept interest rates stable in the quarter. Pursuant to its dual mandate, the committee emphasized the need to be attentive to both inflation and growth risks. In recent remarks, Chair Powell stated the Fed is well positioned to act as needed but also discussed an overall lack of confidence in near-term economic forecasts.

European equities returned 10.64% in the quarter, as reflected in the MSCI Europe Index (USD terms, 6.38% in local currency). Country returns were mostly positive in Europe, reflecting improved investor sentiment amid key policy changes and positive revisions in the earnings outlook. Investors responded positively to key fiscal measures, as Germany announced plans to boost investment in infrastructure and security, and European leaders agreed on a significant increase in defense spending. European private sector data showed improvement, with services seeing higher demand while the manufacturing sector showed one of the largest jumps in output since 2022. Euro area inflation eased modestly over recent months, giving the ECB additional policy flexibility. The headline rate edged down to 2.2% recently while the core rate remains above target levels. From a monetary perspective, the ECB cut rates twice during the quarter while acknowledging that policy is becoming less restrictive. In terms of country performance, Spain and Norway led other markets (in USD terms), returning 22.47% and 21.00%, while Denmark and the Netherlands trailed in the period, returning -11.65% and 1.99%, respectively.

Developed Asia Pacific saw widely mixed returns during the quarter (in USD terms), as the MSCI Pacific Index returned 0.46% (in USD, -3.03% in local currency). Japanese equities rose slightly during the quarter in USD terms. From a macro perspective, retail sales and business confidence dipped recently as new tariff concerns influenced the outlook. In terms of policy, the Bank of Japan raised rates a notch in the quarter and is expected to edge rates higher from their low absolute levels, supported by an improving economy, rising prices, and wages. Australian shares declined in the quarter, as the region processed mixed economic data and concerns about the potential of higher global tariffs. The Reserve Bank cut interest rates once in the quarter but highlighted an uncertain backdrop for domestic activity and inflation. Hong Kong equities rose over 4% in the first quarter. Hong Kong’s economy continued to see muted activity, with recent data reflecting low business confidence, although manufacturing output and new orders rose in the quarter overall. In terms of select country performance (in USD terms), Japan returned 0.31%, while Australia and Hong Kong returned -3.26% and -0.03%, respectively.

Emerging markets outperformed global markets in the first quarter, as the MSCI Emerging Markets Index returned 3.01% (in USD terms, 2.73% in local currency). Emerging markets saw a broad range of returns, as investors evaluated considerable policy uncertainty and mixed growth dynamics. Chinese equities generated strong quarterly returns, reflecting improved economic data and positive earnings releases. China’s economy has remained below its previous growth level, but the loosening of fiscal and monetary policies is supporting a sustained recovery. Stocks in India rallied in March but trailed overall in the first quarter amid higher valuations and capital rotation. India’s economy continued to exhibit solid GDP growth and robust PMI data in services and manufacturing activity. In terms of policy, the Reserve Bank of India lowered its interest rate by a quarter point during the quarter, marking the first reduction since 2020. Among individual EM countries, China (+15.06%) and Brazil (+14.12%) led in USD terms, while the laggards included Taiwan (-12.56%) and Indonesia (-11.03%).

Currency. The DXY Dollar Index declined -3.94% in the first quarter. The index measures the performance of the US dollar against a basket of major world currencies. The euro (+4.46%), British pound (+3.21%), Swiss franc (+2.61%), and the Japanese yen (+4.83%) each appreciated versus the dollar in the period.

Performance Review

International equities led global markets in the first quarter, experiencing a wide range of returns across regions and sectors. Investors navigated a highly rotational environment marked by heightened policy uncertainty, slowing global growth, and a weaker US dollar.

The Calamos International Growth Strategy notched a small gain in the quarter but trailed its indices. Specifically, the strategy returned 0.54% ( gross of fees) and 0.31% (net of fees) versus the 5.36% return of the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index and 2.21% return of the MSCI EAFE Growth Index. The portfolio’s sector allocation and relative security selection detracted value as many of our companies with higher growth and quality fundamentals were not rewarded in the broader market environment.

Positive Influences on Performance

Industrials. The portfolio’s leading security selection and average overweight position in industrials, notably aerospace & defense, boosted relative returns. In addition, our lack of representation in human resource & employment services assisted return.

Materials. Leading security selection in materials, specifically in the gold and construction materials industries, contributed to performance.

Negative Influences on Performance

Information Technology. Over the period, security selection and an average overweight stance within the information technology sector weakened return. In particular, positions in the application software and semiconductor materials & equipment industries lagged.

Consumer Discretionary. Security selection and an average overweight position within the apparel, accessories & luxury goods and casinos & gaming industries of the consumer discretionary sector lost ground on a relative basis.

Positioning and Portfolio Changes

The strategy’s regional and country positioning reflects the combined inputs of our top-down global framework and our bottom-up security analysis. Our investment team evaluates macroeconomic factors and growth opportunities, actively integrating them into the investment decision-making process.

  • Regarding portfolio positioning, we see a range of opportunities across secular growth, select cyclicals, and positions with more defensive growth characteristics.
  • From a sector perspective, technology, industrials, consumer discretionary, and financials represent significant weights in the portfolio. We own key industry positions in application software, semiconductors and equipment, diversified banks, aerospace & defense, industrial conglomerates, broadline retail, and restaurants.
  • We are underweight in energy, utilities, health care, and real estate.
  • In Europe we hold a blend of secular growth, cyclicals, and higher-quality defensives. We continue to evaluate the impact of fiscal stimulus measures and demand, given the region’s extensive linkages to global trade.
  • We own a range of holdings in emerging markets. Positioning reflects our view of key policy reforms, an increase in local consumption, and attractive valuations.
  • We see multiple opportunities in Japan, with an emphasis on companies benefiting from regulatory and governance reforms, in addition to exposure to key innovation industries.

Allocations to financials and industrials rose during the period with increased weights in diversified banks and aerospace & defense. By contrast, allocations to information technology and consumer staples decreased over the period with reductions to semiconductor materials & equipment.

Outlook

International equities continue to face a set of diverse crosscurrents. We are analyzing multiple market drivers, including growth and inflation dynamics, tariff and policy considerations, and monetary conditions. With the US administration challenging the status quo globally and continually shifting secular growth tailwinds, we expect this year to be a period to capitalize on disruption. Within this environment, we continue to identify a range of opportunities at the thematic, regional, and market-cap levels.

For additional information, please visit the strategy’s profile page:

https://www.calamos.com/strategies/international-growth/

data.title: Intellicheck: A Small-Cap SaaS Name Tackling A Big Problem
data.description:
Intellicheck, Inc. (NASDAQ:IDN) isn’t a name I had on my radar until recently, but I think it deserves a lot more attention than it gets. The company sits in a niche part of the SaaS market (identity verification and ID authentication), but its value proposition is hard to ignore, especially in a landscape where fraud and compliance pressures are only intensifying.

After going through its Q1 2025 earnings report and transcript, the main thing that stands out to me is how consistent and focused the business is. Revenue is growing, gross margins remain firmly above 90%, and losses are reducing with each passing quarter. But even more importantly, the business model is evolving, and it’s steadily embedding itself into adjacent verticals like financial services, automotive, and title insurance. These are all industries where the cost of identity fraud is even higher.

My Buy thesis hinges on the fact that Intellicheck’s expansion into regulated, high-trust industries gives it a long runway for growth. Also, its capital-light, high-margin model provides plenty of leverage as it scales. For a company that trades below the radar, this looks like a business that’s just starting to hit its stride.

Company Snapshot

Intellicheck provides real-time identity authentication and fraud prevention solutions for both physical and digital ID verification, serving sectors like retail, financial services, law enforcement, and government. The company says it scans over 100 million identities annually in North America, which is impressive for a business of its size. However, that is only a tiny fraction of the ID verification and authentication market, so there’s a lot of room to grow here.

It integrates technologies such as ID parsing, barcode scanning, risk-scoring, age/gender estimation, and watchlist checks. All of these are designed to thwart fake credentials and streamline compliance at the point of transaction. It accomplishes this via its Intellicheck platform, which has 4 modes:

  • Mobile

  • Portal

  • Direct

  • Capture

Those cater to different business needs, depending on what the business needs, and we’re seeing more widespread adoption.

Right now, I’d say that the company’s main competition includes players like ID.me and platform providers such as NCR. However, I believe that Intellicheck’s consistent gross margins and niche in physical/digital ID verification give it a differentiated edge, particularly in regulated sectors where fast, accurate validation matters most.

Q1 2025: Another Quietly Strong Quarter

Intellicheck’s Q1 2025 didn’t blow the doors off, but it had the hallmarks of progress that I like to see from a company in its stage of growth: consistent execution, tighter expense control, and just enough top-line growth to show the strategy is working.

Revenue came in at $4.89 million, up ~5% year-over-year, and it was almost entirely driven by SaaS, which made up $4.87 million of that total. That’s a 5.6% increase in software revenue, and it’s meaningful because Intellicheck doesn’t rely on flashy one-time deals. Their revenue is sticky, driven by contract renewals and incremental client expansion.

Margins stayed strong, with gross profit around 90%, which is in line with the company’s historical numbers, and operating expenses dropped slightly, down 1% YoY. That operating discipline has helped to reduce the company’s net loss to just $318,000, or $0.02 per share, while adjusted EBITDA came in nearly flat at -$17,000. That is practically breakeven for a business of this size.

One stat that caught my attention: deferred revenue was up $3.5 million year-over-year, the highest it’s ever been. That’s a quiet but telling signal that customers are renewing and pre-paying at a healthy clip, something I’ll be keeping an eye on as the year unfolds.

No fireworks, but a solid, clean quarter—and just enough to warrant a deeper look at the strategic levers driving the business forward.

Strategic Levers

1. Vertical Expansion and Diversified Revenue Streams

One of the most compelling elements of Intellicheck’s story right now is its successful pivot away from an overreliance on the retail sector. That used to be its dominant vertical, but we’re seeing some challenges there. For example, revenue from traditional retail clients declined by 26% year-over-year in Q1 2025 due to a general consumer malaise. But the beauty here is in the pivot: the company’s growing exposure to other, more resilient verticals offsets this drag.

Today, retail banking and general retail each roughly make up equal portions of revenue, according to management, and it’s a dramatic change from just a year ago. The way I see it, that’s a clear sign of the firm's strategic realignment.

But what is even more impressive is the momentum building we’re seeing in high-value niches like title insurance, where the company is now working with firms that represent approximately 40% of all U.S. title insurance volume. I don’t think we should view these deals as just good wins. They’re more than that because they give Intellicheck a foothold in mission-critical transactions where fraud prevention is essential and the value-per-scan is higher than in legacy retail.

2. A Strong Sales Engine with Early Results

Another very important thing I want to highlight is this: Intellicheck is changing how it sells. The company recently brought on Tim Poulin as Senior Vice President of Sales. This is an executive hire who helped grow one of his previous companies, Ping Identity, from $85 million to over $800 million in sales. And from what I can find in the earnings call, it looks like he hasn’t wasted any time getting to work. So far, he’s already added three new sales associates focused on landing new logos, bringing the team to eight reps focused purely on net new accounts.

That’s not just window dressing—the expanded team is already seeing traction. In Q1 alone, the company secured new business in areas like email account security (expanding into Canada after U.S. and European deployments) and added one of the top U.S. title insurers to its client roster. Even better, another top-tier title company is in proof-of-concept testing, with a national rollout potentially on deck.

3. Platform Modernization and Scalability

I can also say that under the hood, it looks like Intellicheck is hard at work, future-proofing its operations. The company is migrating from Microsoft Azure to Amazon Web Services (AWS). The expectation here is that the move will reduce how much it spends on cloud infrastructure costs, make onboarding faster, and provide improved data feeds for analytics. It already migrated three large clients in April, and there are another 11 slated for the current quarter.

Clearly, then, this tech overhaul isn’t just about efficiency—it’s about scaling intelligently. As I pointed out earlier, Intellicheck processes identity verification data on roughly 100 million people annually in North America. As such, streamlining its backend means that the company can absorb new customer growth without sacrificing margin. Speaking of which, adjusted gross margin for Q1 stood at a stellar 91.8%, which is hard to beat in SaaS land.

4. Reseller and Partner Strategy Taking Shape

When we look beyond direct sales, the company is also increasingly leaning into a channel strategy. Take verticals like title insurance, where many small firms still rely on outdated tools. Here, Intellicheck is working with resellers to scale its reach, and the results don’t lie. In Q1 2025, revenue from the title segment jumped 350% year-over-year, and there’s an even split between direct enterprise deals and those that it sourced through resellers.

If you consider this from a sales perspective, going with the dual-pronged approach allows the company to punch above its weight. This way, it can win high-value enterprise deals while efficiently penetrating the long-tail SMB market through partners. It’s a high-margin, high-leverage strategy that’s just getting started. I like it.

Valuation: This Is a Skinny SaaS With a Small-Cap Premium

The main thing you should consider here is that Intellicheck is a microcap stock. It currently trades with a market capitalization of around $106 million and an enterprise value of $101 million. Having said that, the valuation tells a more forward-thinking story.

Let’s look at the multiples. The stock is trading at about 5.2x trailing revenue, and its forward P/S is closer to 4.9x, going off analyst projections. Both are at a premium to the sector median. Now, the forward P/E ratio is beyond steep, well north of 175x, and that looks almost preposterous if there isn’t any additional context. Remember, though: Intellicheck’s earnings profile is near breakeven. In my view, these figures suggest that the market is already paying for the company’s revenue growth and its nearly 90% gross margin SaaS model, even though free cash flow remains negative.

If we go back to its financials, though, it makes some sense. The company has reported $20.2 million in trailing twelve-month revenue, a ~5–6% YoY growth rate, and gross margins of ~90%. All of those things reinforce my view of just how easily scalable Intellicheck’s evolving business model is. I can also point to the fact that its operating expenses are under control, and the Q1 non-GAAP EBITDA loss shrank to just $17,000.

So yes, Intellicheck is commanding a premium relative to peers, but I posit that it’s reasonable to chalk it down to steady execution, strong margins, and growing adoption across lucrative verticals like title insurance and banking. That makes me confident in the Buy thesis; this is a fundamentally solid SaaS business with room to reward stability and scale.

Risks

Customer concentration still matters

Although I can’t find exact figures, it’s more than likely that a handful of enterprise clients drive a meaningful portion of Intellicheck’s revenue. Consequently, we might see flattened growth for a bit if any of those accounts pull back or delay expansion.

Small-cap execution risk

Until fairly recently, the company has run a very lean team with limited sales bandwidth. What that means is that even now, with the new hires and SVP of sales, execution depends heavily on them working well together and efficiently scaling new verticals. Any missteps could slow momentum.

Market visibility is low

Despite a strong platform, Intellicheck remains under the radar. As I pointed out earlier, it’s a microcap, and that can limit investor liquidity and near-term multiple expansion, even if the fundamentals continue to improve.

Investor Takeaway

Intellicheck does not have a big footprint, but the foundation it is building is remarkably solid. We have 90%+ gross margins, low churn, and growing adoption across fraud-sensitive industries like title insurance, banking, and logistics.

Taking all of these together, what is here is a company that’s executing a focused, high-leverage play in digital ID authentication. I’m especially encouraged by its growing deferred revenue, operational discipline, and early signs that the new sales strategy is landing. Of course, at ~5x sales, it’s not really cheap. But for a capital-light, mission-critical SaaS business with real upside in underserved verticals, it’s a premium I’m willing to pay.

data.title: Touchstone Sands Capital International Growth Equity Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Highlights

  • The Fund seeks long-term growth of capital, investing primarily in equity securities of non-U.S. companies of any size, but generally focuses on larger, more established companies
  • Applies bottom-up security analysis that includes fundamental, sector-based research in seeking to identify businesses that have high or improving returns on capital, barriers to competition and compelling valuations
  • Selects investments based on an evaluation of a company's sustainability practices which considers and analyzes the potential Environmental, Social and Governance impacts and risks of a company, how well the company manages these impacts and risks, and ascertains the company's willingness and ability to take a leadership position in implementing best practices

Market Recap

The first quarter of 2025 presented a complex landscape for international equities. Overall Value outperformed Growth during the quarter (as measured by the MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. Growth and Value Indexes) and by the widest margin since early 2022.

Additionally, results were divergent across developed and emerging markets.

Within developed markets, European equities experienced a notable upswing during the quarter. The anticipation of a ceasefire in Ukraine significantly boosted investor confidence, propelling Germany's DAX (DAX:IND) and the pan-continental Euro STOXX indices to record highs. This optimism was further supported by substantial defense spending initiatives across the continent. In particular, German equities experienced one of the best starts to a year since German reunification in 1990. The Euro STOXX 600 Index (STOXX) ended the quarter up over 7% compared to a nearly 5% decline for the S&P 500 (SP500), (SPX).

In contrast, Asian developed markets faced headwinds. Japan's Nikkei 225 (NKY:IND) and South Korea's KOSPI (KOSPI) indices declined by nearly 10%, driven by concerns over impending U.S. tariffs affecting major trading partners.

Looking at emerging markets, Chinese equities demonstrated resilience, with Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index (HSI) surging 16% year-to-date. This growth was underpinned by stronger corporate profits, advancements in artificial intelligence (AI), and a more accommodating regulatory environment. Notably, local AI startup DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) invigorated investments in the Technology sector, with major companies like Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY), Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF), and Alibaba (BABA) reporting robust earnings.

However, broader emerging markets faced challenges due to trade tensions. The U.S. administration's tariff strategies raised concerns about economic growth, particularly affecting commodity exporters reliant on Chinese demand.

From a country perspective, China and Germany were the largest contributors to the MSCI ACWI ex-U.S.' results, while Taiwan and India were the top detractors. From a sector perspective, Financials and Industrials were the top contributors, while Information Technology and Real Estate were the top detractors.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Sands Capital International Growth Fund (MUTF:TPYYX) (Class A Shares, Load Waived) underperformed the MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. Index for the quarter ended March 31, 2025.

The Fund underperformed the MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. year-to-date, as growth stocks globally are on pace to underperform value stocks by the widest margin since 2000 (as measured by the MSCI ACWI Growth vs. MSCI ACWI Value). The risk-off environment created by the possibility of a global trade war no doubt drove investors to shorter-term safe havens.

The Fund's decline is attributed to multiple compression, as the Fund delivered earnings growth for the quarter that exceeded the MSCI ACWI ex-U.S.Security selection was the primary detractor from the Fund's relative results and overwhelmed contributions from regional allocation. From a regional perspective, Eastern Europe and Emerging Asia were the top contributors to relative results, while Western Europe and the U.S./Canada were the top detractors. From a sector perspective, Communication Services and Consumer Staples were the top contributors, while Industrials and Consumer Discretionary were the top detractors.

Among the top absolute contributors to investment results during the quarter included Sea Ltd. (SE) (Singapore; Communication Services sector), Spotify Technology SA (SPOT) (Sweden; Communication Services sector), MercadoLibre Inc. (MELI) (Argentina; Consumer Discretionary sector), Bajaj Finance Ltd. (India; Financials sector), and CTS Eventim AG & Co. (OTCPK:CEVMF) (Germany; Communication Services sector).

Sea is an internet business in Southeast Asia that operates leading platforms for video games, ecommerce, and digital financial services. The business reported its fifth consecutive beat-and-raise quarter, with Shopee (ecommerce) growing gross merchandise volume 24% year-over-year while delivering positive EBITDA margin. 2025 guidance points to inflections across all three business units, ecommerce, digital financial services, and gaming, each expected to generate $1 billion in EBITDA. We raised our long-term estimates, including Shopee margin and valuation assumptions, and now expect combined EBITDA to triple by 2029. Improved investor communication has, in our view, helped dampen volatility around earnings announcements while attracting long-term institutional investors. Looking ahead, we believe the company is more in control of its destiny and entering a phase of meaningful earnings acceleration, and we have higher confidence in our long-term earnings estimates.

Spotify is the world's largest subscription streaming audio service by market share. The business delivered strong fourth-quarter 2024 results, with robust revenue and monthly active user growth, along with gross and operating margin expansion. It also reported its strongest quarter of premium subscription net additions in five years. Management is calling 2025 the "year of accelerated execution" following 2024's "year of monetization." We view this as a positive signal of continued operating expense discipline and product innovation, including the launch of a new premium "Music Pro" tier.

Bajaj Finance is a leading Indian nonbank financial services company. The business reported strong quarterly results, with loan growth exceeding its long-term target of 25%, despite management's decision to reduce disbursements across several products amid India's ongoing asset quality cycle. Most notably, Bajaj's asset quality showed signs of stabilization as delinquencies declined. We expect this to reduce credit costs-elevated in recent quarters-and support improved profitability. During the quarter, Bajaj also announced the formal transition of Managing Director from Rajeev Jain to Anup Saha. Mr. Jain will remain Vice Chair of Bajaj Finance, which we expect to help ease concerns about a disruptive leadership change.

Among the top absolute detractors to investment results included Recruit Holdings Co. (OTCPK:RCRRF) (Japan; Industrials sector), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) (Taiwan; Information Technology sector), Flutter Entertainment plc (FLUT) (Ireland; Consumer Discretionary sector), Shopify Inc. (SHOP) (Canada; Information Technology sector), and Pandora AS (OTCPK:PNDZF) (Denmark; Consumer Discretionary sector).

Recruit is a leading global human resources technology business. Recruit shares rose after delivering solid results, with revenue up 4% and adjusted EBITDA and earnings growing 14% and 18% year over year. HR Technology led growth, driven by higher revenue per job post and expanding margins. Additionally, the upcoming launch of an AI-powered career counselor aims to enhance job seeker experience, which could enhance Recruit's overall ecosystem. Marketing Solutions grew 7%, while Staffing remained steady, with strength in Japan offsetting softer trends overseas.

Taiwan Semiconductor (TSMC) is the world's largest producer of leading-edge logic chips by market share. Shares declined as part of the broader correction in AI-related infrastructure stocks. Despite near-term volatility, we continue to view TSMC as a key beneficiary of AI chip demand. Revenue from AI accelerators more than tripled in 2024, accounting for 15% of total revenue. Management expects this figure to double again in 2025 and grow at a mid- 40% annual rate over the next five years. While the rise of models like DeepSeek has raised concerns about demand, we believe greater AI accessibility will drive increased need for advanced semiconductor manufacturing.

Flutter Entertainment is the world's leading sports-betting and gaming business by revenue. Flutter pre-announced weaker U.S. results for the fourth quarter, driven by unusually customer friendly sports outcomes in December. U.S. revenue and adjusted EBITDA came in 19% and 56% below guidance, respectively, reflecting a tough NFL season with the highest rate of favorites (teams or players widely expected to win) winning in two decades. While this volatility impacted margins, it is an inherent feature of the business and, in our view, increasingly understood by investors. Importantly, structural U.S. margins still expanded 100 basis points year over year to a record 14.5%, supported by growth in higher-margin parlay bets. Outside the U.S., results were stronger, with full-year revenue and EBITDA exceeding guidance on momentum in the UK and favorable Premier League results. We continue to view Flutter as a leading global operator with strong long-term fundamentals.

There were no purchases or sales made in the quarter.

Outlook and Conclusion

We expect 2025 to include periods of significant volatility as markets react to developments in AI, geopolitical events, and shifts in the global economy. Market structure, particularly the growing influence of hedge funds in incremental trading flows, may further amplify this dynamic.

We do not claim to forecast news or short-term data, or how markets will immediately respond. What we do claim is the ability to understand business models and identify the drivers of above-average earnings growth. While sentiment shapes short-term price movements, long-term returns are ultimately driven by the compounding of earnings.

A core principle of our investment philosophy is the belief that we know something about a business's future that the market does not. That difference of opinion can create near-term volatility, but also meaningful opportunity. Our differentiated business insights and long investment horizon are structural advantages that can help tilt the odds in our favor.

Today, markets appear focused on near-term news flow. What may be underappreciated are the broader shifts that matter more for long-term investors. Strong demand for AI tools, declining costs, and tangible use cases-many of which are already generating revenue or cost savings across our Fund. Ongoing industry consolidation and formalization globally, often benefiting existing market leaders and further entrenching their competitive positions. The emergence of new and improved products and services that are delivering meaningful value to users while displacing the status quo.

While we do not dismiss the potential for an economic or earnings recession, we believe the Fund today is better positioned than it was in 2021 for economic and market uncertainty. It is more diversified across end markets, sectors, countries, and growth stages, with greater earnings visibility and stability. Key Fund metrics, profitability and valuation, have also improved without sacrificing long-term growth potential.

Long-term investing does not mean "set it and forget it." We continue to monitor inputs and supply chains to assess potential risk and can act quickly if needed, whether exiting impaired businesses or leaning into mispriced opportunities. We remain confident in the Fund and process to help create long-term value.

We cannot predict how the second quarter, or the remainder of 2025, will unfold. However, our track record demonstrates our ability to forecast the direction and magnitude of long-term earnings growth. We remain confident in the Fund's portfolio of leading growth businesses and in our time-tested investment process to deliver long-term value.

data.title: Bank of Hawaii Series A: Cyclicality Risk Remains But Strong Long-Term Upside Potential
data.description:
Bank of Hawaii Corporation 4.375% DEP PFD A (NYSE:BOH.PR.A) is covered within this article. If you've followed our content for an extended period, you'd probably be familiar with the fact that we've covered a series of discounted REIT preferred shares in recent months. However, here we shift focus by covering a banking preferred share, which, we believe, could provide healthy long-term returns. While interim stumbles are possible, we think gradual reinvestment into Bank of Hawaii's (BOH) Series A will yield long-term benefits; here's why.

Overview of Series A

Bank of Hawaii's Series A shares are non-cumulative preferred shares, meaning skipped dividends won't necessarily be repaid in the future. That said, the issuance is perpetual, trading at a discount, has a lowish obligation, and a compelling current yield. Bank of Hawaii's call date remains distant but regardless of that fact, we don't see call risk playing much of a role in for now given that Series A is at a noteworthy discount.

Variable Value
Market Price 26/06/25' $15.59
Liquidation Value $25.00
Current Yield 7.02%
Initial Coupon 4.375%
Call Date 08/01/2026

Source: Preferred Stock Channel

The bank issued its Series A shares in 2021. Unfortunately, the issuance has traded at a discount for most of its tenure. Generally speaking, 2022-2023 was a rough period for banks as a credit scare occurred, leading to the failure of notable banks such as SVB and Signature Bank (OTC:SBNY). Bank of Hawaii's Series A issuance probably felt a systematic pinch. Although it has stabilised ever since, Series A remains discounted.

I had previously mentioned that Bank of Hawaii's Series A isn't cumulative. However, as illustrated below, the shares have a solid repayment history, surviving a stress period in 2022/23. This doesn't guarantee future repayment, but it certainly provides a worthwhile scenario.

Fundamental Take

Considering the fact that we are discussing a preferred share issuance and not common stock, most of the fundamental talking points are from a counter-party risk perspective instead of a growth perspective. Some investors might disagree with the angle, but we decided to structure it this way as a matter of preference - In our view, preferred shares don't share in growth, they rely on the creditworthiness of incremental dividends.

So, let's traverse into a few fundamental talking points.

Deposits remain stable at the bank. Most of Bank of Hawaii's deposits are interest-bearing, which okay, seeing as lending rates are also elevated. We don't see any funding concerns arising unless short-term rates remain pent-up during a recession, in which case, we'd probably see lower net interest income.

Further to the above, the bank's deposits have repriced lower, partially explaining why its net interest income has expanded. Many loans will have been locked-in at high rates in the past few years, and lowering rates might further enhance NII margins as fundings costs drop. A limiting factor here might be that lower rates could lead to higher volatility in deposits, especially if a recession had to occur.

Now that we've discussed the bank's liability level, let's look at its profit & loss and balance sheet metrics. The bank's income illustrates progressive return on assets, return on equity, net interest margins, among other ratios. We've argued for months that the credit environment might deteriorate slightly in the coming quarters. While such a scenario might impact the Bank of Hawaii's common stockholders, we don't think it would derail the bank to a degree that it would be unable to repay, especially considering the bank's strong baseline figures shown below. In addition, Bank of Hawaii has 53% of its deposits insured, and it posted a tier 1 capital ratio of 13.93% and total capital ratio of 14.97%, well-above regulatory standards required by the Basel Committee.

Non-performing assets, charge-offs, and delinquencies have been reported as stable. We see this as a significant bonus for preferred stock investors. Again, we reiterate that the credit cycle is cyclical and likely to diminish in the face of a slowing economy. That said, we don't see Bank of Hawaii developing solvency issues.

Risks

Credit Spreads

Credit spread widening is a risk to both banks and preferred shares in general. The prior because wider spreads signal higher credit and thus counter-party risk; the latter because preferred shares fall lower on the capital structure, meaning they're more likely to default than senior bonds, drawn revolving credit lines, and so forth. In the event that credit spreads 'blow out', we could see a repeat of 2022/23 where Bank of Hawaii's Series A shed telling value after spreads widened.

The Bank's Cyclicality

A bank is a cyclical vehicle, it's essentially a leveraged bet on the economy. Fortunately, the Bank of Hawaii's consumer portfolio banks on high FICO scores and moderate exposure to auto debt (which can be very cyclical and prone to refinancing). We see this as a positive, likely explaining why the bank's senior debt has an A rating from Moody's (MCO).

On the other hand, around 44% of the bank's debt is commercial. Risk premiums on commercial real estate and construction can be extremely volatile, and therefore we flag this as a risk, considering the economy's fragility.

Conclusion

Bank of Hawaii Corporation 4.375% DEP PFD A is a risky bet at this stage of the economic cycle, especially considering its non-cumulative nature. However, at a discount of circa 37%, and a yield of 7%+, we think it's a good time to start buying into the Series As, particularly as the Bank of Hawaii's core variables suggest minor solvency risk, even if we were to head into a credit cycle deteriorating.

This doesn't seem like a short-term tactical play. Instead, a position in Bank of Hawaii Corporation 4.375% DEP PFD A would likely yield longer-term benefits, contingent on the ability to incrementally re-invest if drawdowns had to occur.

Consensus: Bullish
